coredata.dc:title: Anti-counterfeiting application of fluorescent carbon dots derived from wasted coffee grounds
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Woo Tae
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description: Anti-counterfeiting application have various challenges and issue of the luminescent materials which remain to be resolved, such as expensive cost, low synthetic yield and photo-bleaching effect. Since the carbon dots (CDs) derived from wasted organic materials exhibits cheap synthetic cost, noble photo-stability and high synthetic yield, CDs has been paid attention in the anti-counterfeiting field. Herein, we report the synthesis of the fluorescent carbon dots derived from wasted coffee ground and their anti-counterfeiting application. The CDs prepared by hydrothermal synthesis shows uniform morphology with lattice structure. Also, the prepared CDs shows a hydrophilic nature and blue emission under UV excitation. In order to apply a CDs in the anti-counterfeiting field, the resultant CDs ink was printed on various banknotes, by using a simple spray method. The anti-counterfeiting patterns exhibits a blue emission under UV excitation and excellent covertness under daylight with high resolution. These results indicate that the CDs derived from wasted coffee grounds can be applied as a promising luminescent materials in the anti-counterfeiting field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Wasted coffee grounds
Anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images and (c) size distribution of CDs.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra (b) C1s (c) N1s and (d) O1s scan of CDs.
(a) UV–vis and PL spectra (at dominant excitation and emission wavelength) of CDs, (b) PL spectra of CDs as a function of excitation wavelength.
Anti-counterfeiting pattern printed on the various Korean banknotes under daylight and 365 nm excitation.

head.ce:title: Anti-counterfeiting application of fluorescent carbon dots derived from wasted coffee grounds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Program of LED and Solid State Lighting Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Program of LED and Solid State Lighting Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interdisciplinary Program of LED and Solid State Lighting Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anti-counterfeiting application have various challenges and issue of the luminescent materials which remain to be resolved, such as expensive cost, low synthetic yield and photo-bleaching effect. Since the carbon dots (CDs) derived from wasted organic materials exhibits cheap synthetic cost, noble photo-stability and high synthetic yield, CDs has been paid attention in the anti-counterfeiting field. Herein, we report the synthesis of the fluorescent carbon dots derived from wasted coffee ground and their anti-counterfeiting application. The CDs prepared by hydrothermal synthesis shows uniform morphology with lattice structure. Also, the prepared CDs shows a hydrophilic nature and blue emission under UV excitation. In order to apply a CDs in the anti-counterfeiting field, the resultant CDs ink was printed on various banknotes, by using a simple spray method. The anti-counterfeiting patterns exhibits a blue emission under UV excitation and excellent covertness under daylight with high resolution. These results indicate that the CDs derived from wasted coffee grounds can be applied as a promising luminescent materials in the anti-counterfeiting field.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting is important problem due to its wide impact on many customers, companies and governments [1]. In this respect, the demand on anti-counterfeiting that make genuine items harder to copy and easier to identify have being increased on important and valuable products, including the banknote, luxurious items, ticket, public certificates and documents [2]. During the past decades, various anti-counterfeiting techniques such as printing a luminescent material, recent magnetic response and plasmonic security labels have been studied [3–6]. Among mentioned them, printing luminescent materials possess easy handling, high resolution, fast printing speed, diverse design and versatile application in various object [7,8]. The emission properties of traditional luminescent materials in anti-counterfeiting technique can be obtained by printing certain substitutes or simply mixing different fluorescent materials, which leads poor anti-counterfeiting performance [9,10]. For these reason, developing noble luminescent materials with high covert and reliable properties is highly demanded to prohibit a counterfeiting.

Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), discovered nano-sized carbon materials comprising crystalline graphitic cores, are considered as potential fluorescent materials in photo-electric applications, such as solid state lighting, forensics, anti-counterfeiting, analytical chemistry, biochemistry and medical fields [11–20]. In the anti-counterfeiting technique, CDs exhibits useful advantages, in particular high quantum yield and noble fluorescent stability, cheap and abundant source.

To maximize the advantages of CDs, optimizing its fluorescent properties and covertness are necessary. According to past researches, these properties can be varied by synthesis condition of CDs, such as carbon source, surface functionalization, pH, solvent, purification method. Among various conditions, the carbon source of CDs is important conditions which determine structural, morphological and luminescent characteristics of CDs. Among the natural materials, coffee is one of the most popular drink and the world coffee market has grown. According to the trade statistics of international coffee organization, 10,140,000 tons of coffee bean was consumed from 2018 to 2019 [21]. Because wasted coffee grounds with high waste ratio (up to 99.5 wt. %) is generated after the roasting and extracting process, the demand of recycling the wasted coffee grounds is rising problem from economic and environmental field. Up to now, the wasted coffee grounds were recycled as a medium of mushrooms production, nutrient of earthworms for vermicompost, carbon source of activated carbon, ethanol and biogas [22–25]. Nonetheless, these recycling application is not applied in large scale, due to the generation of toxic gas and salt accumulation by caffeine [26,27]. Since their poor recycling scale, a new recycling method of wasted coffee grounds is important issue.

Considered with roasting and extracting process of wasted coffee grounds, using a wasted coffee grounds make easy to synthesize carbon or graphene compositions. In accordance with these advantage, utilization of wasted coffee grounds has been paid attention to the synthesis of CDs as a carbon source [28–31]. According to Xu’s study, the CDs derived by wasted coffee grounds has been synthesized by using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [28]. The synthesized CDs exhibits rich surface traps and oxygen functional groups applied to graphene membrane for filtering heavy metal ions and organic compounds. Wang and co-workers studied bio-imaging and sensing application of CDs originated from coffee grounds [29]. The CDs functionalized with poly-ethylene imine exhibits low cytotoxicity, highly efficient fluorescent property and high sensitivity to Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions. In our knowledge, anti-counterfeiting application of the CDs derived from wasted coffee grounds as fluorescent materials has not been reported.

In this study, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent CDs derived from wasted coffee grounds. Using wasted coffee grounds as carbon source and de-ionized (D.I.) water as reaction medium, the blue emitting CDs were synthesized with hydrophilic properties. This allowed us to systematically investigate the distinctive PL properties of the fabricated CDs. Moreover, the potential of the blue emitting CDs ink in anti-counterfeiting was explored.

We used wasted coffee grounds as carbon precursor and nitric acid and iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3··9H2O, 99.9 %, Aldrich) as oxidizing agent and catalyst within hydrothermal method. The initial solution prepared by dispersing 1 g of wasted coffee powder, 0.76 g of iron nitrate and 1 mL of nitric acid (HNO3, 70 %, Ducksan chemical) in 40 mL of D.I. water. The initial solutions were stirred with a magnetic stirring for 10 min, and then moved 60 mL of Teflon vessel and placed in stainless steel autoclave. Hydrothermal synthesis was performed at 220 ℃ for 20 h. After that, the initial solution changed from transparent to yellow as a result of formation of CDs. Subsequently, the synthesized solution was purified by using a membrane filtration, centrifuge and sonication process. The 40 mL of purified solution was further dialyzed against D.I. water with molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da. By using a freeze-drying, the 0.197 g of CDs powder was obtained.

In order to utilize the CDs to form a liquid ink types and prevent the luminescence quenching by aggregation, the PVA was dissolved in the CDs solution. The 1.2 g of PVA powder was dissolved into 40 mL of CDs solution heat in boiling water with a magnetic stirring. After the dissolution of PVA in CDs solution, transparent CDs ink with yellowish color was formed. The CDs ink was sprayed on Korean banknotes with patterned mesh by using an airbrush (GAB-01, GSHOBBY) attached on air compressor (MONSTER COMP-001, Airfactory).

The morphological properties of CDs were obtained using a field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100 F) with copper grid. In order to analyze a composition and structural properties of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo VG Scientific, MultiLab 2000). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a FT-IR spectrometer (JASCO, FT/IR-6700). Raman spectroscopy of CDs was carried out using a Raman spectrometer (JASCO, NRS-5100) with 785 nm laser. The absorption properties of CDs were recorded using an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (JASCO, V-670). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs was recorded using a time resolved spectrometer (PTI, PTI system). The anti-counterfeiting images of CDs ink were photographed using a digital camera (DSLR Canon EOS 100D) with 5 mm lens (SIGMA MACRO, 50 mm, F2.8, EXDG), under daylight and 365 nm irradiation.

The size distribution and morphology of CDs synthesized under the optimal hydrothermal synthesis conditions were observed by FE-TEM. Fig. 1 (a), (b) and (c) are TEM image, high resolution TEM image and size distribution of the CDs, respectively. Fig. 1 (a) shows that the CDs possess uniform and spherical shape with good dispersion. Fig. 1 (b) indicate that CDs are in crystalline structures, which confirms that the possessing of crystalline properties with graphitic carbon structure with (1120) direction [17]. The size distribution of CDs was analyzed by a single Gaussian function, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The CDs exhibit a narrow size distribution in range of 0.5–4.0 nm centered at 2.18 nm.

Fig. 2 shows (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of the CDs. The XRD patterns of CDs powder shows single broad peak at 2θ = 21°, which is corresponds to the inter-layer distance of 0.42 nm. The wasted coffee grounds contain various organic compounds such as caffeine, fatty acid, cellulose, hemicellulose, protein and polysaccharides [32]. These compounds have hydrophilic ligand such as −OH, −COOH, CO and –NH2, which possess the high extraction in the hydrothermal process. The formation mechanism of the CDs is carbonization of these organic compounds by carbonization in the wasted coffee grounds [33]. After carbonization, the CDs possess a crystallized honeycomb lattice structure. The Fig. 2(b) shows the presence of broad absorption band from 3000 to 3500 cm−1, which is related with characteristic absorption of −OH and –NH2 stretching vibration [34,35]. The existence of −OH and –NH2 would provide hydrophilic property of CDs. The absorption peaks at 2857 and 2930 cm−1 are originated to stretching vibration of C–H in aromatic rings [36]. The peak at 1696 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bonding. The peak at 1596 cm−1 is corresponded with stretching vibrations of CC and CN bonds which is related to the aromatic bonding of CDs [37]. The characteristic absorption peak of CN at around 1450 cm-1 indicate a substitution of nitrogen in the CDs [38]. The absorption peaks at 1375, 1224, 1154 and 1048 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of phenolic COH bonding, COH bonding in tertiary alcohol and COC bonding, respectively [39]. These results show that the chemical bonds of CDs are formed by aromatization and condensation process of the organic compounds.

Fig. 3 is the Raman spectra of the CDs. The characteristic D and G band were detected at 1361 and 1593 cm−1. The D band is related with edge distortion and structural defect in graphene structure. By surface passivation and nitrogen substitution, the two-dimensional structure of CDs can be distorted. G band is attributed to sp2 carbon structure of graphene structure. During a hydrothermal process, condensation between organic compounds with aromatic or honeycomb-like structure form the CDs with graphitized two-dimensional core structure. The intensity of functionalized edges is characterized by ID/IG ratio of Raman spectra where the effect of functional groups on the surface is suppressed. The calculated ID/IG ratio is 0.76. This results indicate that highly graphitization and surface passivation of CDs were performed.

The chemical bonding and element analysis for the as-synthesized CDs were analyzed by measuring XPS spectra. Fig. 4 is XPS spectra in (a) full range and high resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s spectra of CDs. In Fig. 4(a), the XPS spectra shows three peaks at 284.8, 400 and 532.1 eV, which are correspond to C1s, N1s and O1s. The existence ratio of C, N and O in CDs are 73.0, 18.8 and 7.50 %, respectively. This results signifies that the CDs are composed of C, N, and O elements. The Gaussian fitting of C1s spectra reveals the formation of sp2 carbon (CC and CC; 284.9 eV) binding, carbon-nitrogen (CN and CN; 286.2 eV) binding, carbonyl (CO; 286.8 eV) binding and carboxylate (OC = O; 288.1 eV) binding at the surface of CDs, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The pyridinic (399.7 eV) and graphitic (401.4 eV) nitrogen were observed in Fig. 4(c) which suggest a substitution of nitrogen in the CDs. At Fig. 4(d), O1s spectra calculated to sum of three Gaussian peaks, those peaks are corresponding to functionalized group (528.9 eV), single (531.4 eV) and double bond (532.4 eV) with aromatic carbon [40,41].

To study the optical properties of the CDs, UV–vis absorption and PL properties were analyzed in detail. Fig. 5 (a) is UV–vis spectra and photoluminescence spectra. The absorption band of CDs were observed at 270 nm, which is related with absorption of reduced graphene oxide [42]. The dominant emission and excitation wavelength were recorded to 411 nm and 334 nm, respectively. The Gaussian fitting of the dominant excitation spectra shows that the CDs exhibits two excitation bands; one at 247 nm (π→π* transition of aromatic structure, 4.96 eV) and the other at 334 nm (n→π* transition of surface defects, 3.71 eV). According to Pan’s study, the luminescence of CDs occurs from the radiative relaxation of the excited electrons from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the triplet state of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [43]. As the energy gap between σ1 and π1 state is below 1.50 eV, the CDs have triplet ground states (σ1π1). In this study, the energy gap between two excitation bands is computed 1.26 eV, which indicates that the ground state of CDs is degenerated triplet state. Since the strong reduction of hydrothermal process, the number of cluster-like states from small and isolated sp2 domains are formed, resulting the electron-hole recombination among these cluster-like state exhibits large bandgap [44]. Compared with UV–vis spectra and PL excitation spectra, the transition from sp2 cluster-like state to surface state appear to the most excited electron, while less electron at sp2 cluster-like states lead to excitation state. Since the excitation light induced to the CDs, the excitation from ground state to upper energy state formed by graphene oxide and aromatic carbon structure. After that, most of excited electron transferred to lower energy state formed by surface defects of CDs. The excitons were recombined and PL spectra of CDs shows blue emission centered at 411 nm. Fig. 5 (b) is excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence spectra. Since the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 460 nm, center of PL emission peak was shifted from 404 to 500 nm. These emission spectra are ensemble of energy sub-states due to the presence of multi-chromophoric groups on CDs surface. The distribution of multi-chromophoric group can be changed by dipole moment of solvent, composition of carbon source [45,46].

In order to confirm the capability of CDs ink for anti-counterfeiting application, strong luminescence and capable covertness property on each substrate are critical requirements to disturb and identify a counterfeiting. The anti-counterfeiting patterns can be categorized to the visible and invisible patterns, under daylight [47,48]. Among these patterns, the invisible pattern possesses high security levels because their invisibility makes difficult to counterfeit valuable products. The CDs ink sprayed on various Korean banknote, as shown in Fig. 6 . Compared with normal banknote, the printed pattern exhibits a blue emission under 365 nm light excitation with high resolution, irrespective of background color. The anti-counterfeiting patterns sprayed on various colored banknote (including a blue colored banknote) possess high covertness in daylight. A high covertness of these pattern can extend the applicability of the CDs ink as anti-counterfeiting ink. These properties show that the CDs originated from wasted coffee grounds can be performed important role as a next candidate of luminescent anti-counterfeiting ink with strong luminescence, higher covertness.

In this study, luminescent the carbon dots (CDs) were hydrothermally synthesized from wasted coffee grounds. The morphological and structural analysis of the prepared CDs showed uniform morphology, hydrophilic nature with graphitic structure due to the hydrothermal process. The CDs emit blue light under UV excitation because of the existence of sp2 domain and multifunctional ligand on the CDs. The anti-counterfeiting application of CDs were demonstrated by spraying a CDs ink on the various banknote. Under UV excitation, the anti-counterfeiting pattern shows a blue colored image and high covertness with good resolution. These attractive features can motivate the green and sustainable synthesis of fluorescent CDs in the anti-counterfeiting application.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT; Ministry of Science and ICT) (2020R1A2C400219311).
